description.task: Implement Merge Sort from scratch using the divide-and-conquer paradigm. Track the number of splits and merges to illustrate the recursion depth. Sort an array and print step counts.
instructions: Merge sort splits the array into halves (O(log n) levels) and merges them back
For n=7 elements: splits=6, merges=6 (each element is its own leaf after 6 splits breaking down to pairs/singles)
Time complexity O(n log n), space O(n) for auxiliary arrays

title: Binary Search — Divide and Conquer Recursion

description.task: Implement recursive binary search using divide-and-conquer. Track recursion depth. Also implement power(base, exp) using fast exponentiation (also divide and conquer), reducing multiplications from O(n) to O(log n).
instructions: binarySearch: if(target<arr[mid]) return binarySearch(arr,target,l,mid-1); else return binarySearch(arr,target,mid+1,r);
arr has 10 elements, 13 is at index 6 — depth should be 4 (steps: mid=4,mid=7,mid=5,mid=6)
Fast exponentiation: exp=10→5→2→1→0, only O(log n) multiplications

description.task: Implement the maximum subarray problem using divide and conquer (O(n log n) approach). Split the array at mid, find max crossing subarray, then return max of left, right, and crossing. Compare result with Kadane's O(n) approach.
instructions: D&C runs in O(n log n); Kadane's in O(n) — both give the correct answer
maxCrossing expands left from mid and right from mid+1 to find the best split
description.expectedOutput:
DC result:     6
Kadane result: 6
Same answer:   true
